sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content:
Medical records serve as the legal and clinical account of every patient interaction. They protect patients by ensuring continuity of care, and they protect clinicians by providing a contemporaneous record of assessment, reasoning, and treatment. In remote mine site settings where clinicians may be the sole treating practitioner and where patients may later seek care from other providers, comprehensive records are especially important.

This procedure applies to all patient care documentation generated by clinical staff at FIFO mine site clinics, whether paper-based or electronic. It establishes minimum standards for record creation, storage, access, privacy obligations, and retention. Compliance with the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) and applicable health records legislation is mandatory.

sections.title: Record Creation
sections.content:
Every patient contact must generate a documented record. This includes face-to-face assessments, telehealth consultations, and occupational health encounters. The minimum content of every record entry is:

- Date and time of the encounter
- Patient's full name and date of birth (and patient identifier or employee number if applicable)
- Presenting complaint or reason for encounter
- Relevant history: symptom onset, duration, severity, relevant past medical history, medications, allergies
- Clinical assessment findings: vital signs, physical examination findings relevant to the presentation, functional observations
- Differential diagnoses considered or working diagnosis
- Management plan: treatment given, medications administered (name, dose, route, time), referrals, follow-up instructions
- Disposition: discharged to normal duties, restricted duties, evacuated, admitted, or referred
- Clinician's name, registration number (AHPRA), and signature

Documentation standards:
- Write legibly in indelible ink (for paper records) — poor handwriting that cannot be read is not acceptable
- Never use correction fluid (Tipp-Ex) — draw a single line through errors, initial, and write the correction
- Do not leave blank lines in paper records — line through unused space
- Document in real time or as close to the clinical event as possible — retrospective records must be clearly labelled as such with the time of writing
- Use plain language — avoid abbreviations that are not universally understood or that could be misinterpreted

sections.title: Storage and Access
sections.content:
Medical records must be stored securely at all times:

- Paper records: stored in a lockable filing system within the clinic — access restricted to treating clinical staff and authorised persons
- Electronic records: stored in a password-protected clinical information system with access logging; data should be backed up regularly and encrypted
- Medical records must be stored separately from HR (human resources) employment records — the mine site employer does not have routine access to identifiable clinical records
- Access to an individual's clinical record is restricted to:
  - The treating clinician(s) directly involved in that patient's care
  - Other clinical staff at the site with a legitimate treating relationship
  - The patient themselves (see below)
  - Statutory authorities where disclosure is legally required (e.g., Public Health Act notifications)
  - Workers compensation insurer or employer, but only for the compensable episode and only with the patient's written consent or as required by law

Patient access to their own records:
- Patients have the right to request access to their own health records under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)
- Respond to such requests promptly — within 30 days — and in consultation with the medical director
- Access may be declined only in limited circumstances defined by law (e.g., where access would pose a serious threat to life)

sections.title: Privacy Obligations
sections.content:
Clinical staff are bound by the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) and by professional ethical obligations of confidentiality. Key principles:

- Personal health information may only be collected for a legitimate clinical purpose and with the patient's knowledge
- Health information may only be used for the purpose for which it was collected (treatment) unless the patient consents to another use or disclosure is required by law
- Health information must not be shared with the patient's employer without the patient's explicit written consent — this includes information about diagnoses, medications, or clinical findings
- Exceptions where disclosure is permitted without consent include: mandatory public health reporting (e.g., notifiable diseases — refer to WA Communicable Diseases regulations), disclosure required by a court order or statutory authority, and disclosure necessary to prevent a serious and imminent threat to life
- Workers compensation: for a compensable injury or illness, disclosure to the relevant insurer or employer of information directly related to the compensable condition is generally permitted and may be required — however, broader health information remains protected
- Never discuss patient information in common areas, with non-clinical site staff, or with the patient's colleagues, supervisors, or family without consent
- FIFO settings present unique challenges — maintain confidentiality vigilantly in an environment where patients and clinicians often work and live in close proximity

sections.title: Retention and Disposal
sections.content:
Medical records must be retained for the following minimum periods:

- Adult patient records: minimum 7 years from the date of the last entry
- Records of patients who were minors at the time of treatment: until the patient turns 25 years of age, or 7 years from the last entry, whichever is longer
- If any litigation, complaint, coronial investigation, or regulatory process is pending or anticipated: records must be retained indefinitely until the matter is fully resolved, regardless of the standard retention period

Disposal:
- Records that have reached the end of their retention period may be disposed of, but must be destroyed securely
- Paper records: shred using a cross-cut shredder or engage a secure document destruction service
- Electronic records: overwrite or destroy storage media in accordance with ASD (Australian Signals Directorate) guidance
- Never place medical records in general waste

sections.title: Privacy Breach Response
sections.content:
A privacy breach occurs when personal health information is collected, used, or disclosed in a way that contravenes the Privacy Act or applicable health records legislation. This includes:

- Unauthorised access to records by site staff or management
- Accidental disclosure of a patient's health information to a third party
- Loss or theft of paper records or a device containing electronic records
- Sending records to the wrong recipient (fax, email, post)

Response steps:
1. Contain the breach immediately — retrieve documents if possible, secure devices, change passwords
2. Assess the likely harm to the patient(s) whose information was disclosed
3. Notify the patient(s) affected as soon as practicable
4. Notify the medical director and privacy officer (or equivalent within the organisation) immediately
5. Document the breach in full: what information, how many patients, how it occurred, steps taken
6. Notifiable Data Breaches scheme: under the Privacy Act, breaches likely to result in serious harm to individuals must be notified to the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) — the medical director will advise on this obligation
